Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 32
Recent Green phase: No code change. Claim referencing wrong item type already throws via existing validateDamages logic.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run autonomously, return after completion.

Working directory: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-27_03-39-46_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking

attachment.content:
- red: TDD Red Phase
- green: TDD Green Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. Let me compute PRE measurements.

## PRE Measurements

**ESLint smells: 0** (clean)

**APP mass and McCabe per function:**

| Function | APP mass | McCabe | Cognitive |
|---|---|---|---|
| `insuranceValue` | 1·1 (binding) + 1·2 (subscript+??) = ~3 | 1 | 1 |
| `insuranceSum` | ~10 (1 loop, 2 invocations, 3 bindings) | 1 | 1 |
| `basePremium` | ~3 | 1 | 1 |
| `groupByType` | ~26 (1 loop, 3 invocations, 3 assignments, several bindings) | 1 | 2 |
| `sumBasePremiums` | ~10 | 1 | 1 |
| `isCompleteComponentBlock` | ~10 (1 conditional via && short-circuit, 2 invocations) | 2 | 1 |
| `groupTotal` | ~10 (1 conditional, 1 invocation) | 2 | 1 |
| `policyBase` | ~16 (1 loop, 3 invocations) | 1 | 1 |
| `perItemSurcharge` | ~16 (1 loop, 1 invocation of predicate, 1 ternary) | 2 | 2 |
| `isCursed` | ~3 | 1 | 1 |
| `enchantmentLevel` | ~3 | 1 | 1 |
| `isHighlyEnchanted` | ~5 | 1 | 1 |
| `firstInsuranceSurcharge` | ~4 | 1 | 1 |
| `loyaltyDiscount` | ~12 (ternary) | 2 | 1 |
| `followupDiscount` | ~9 (ternary) | 2 | 1 |
| `totalSurcharges` | ~14 (3 invocations) | 1 | 1 |
| `totalDiscounts` | ~10 (2 invocations) | 1 | 1 |
| `quotePremium` | ~17 | 1 | 1 |
| `processQuote` | ~22 (many invocations, 1 assignment) | 1 | 1 |
| `reimbursementRate` | ~7 (ternary) | 2 | 1 |
| `damagePayout` | ~15 (1 invocation .find, 1 type cast, arithmetic) | 1 | 1 |
| `countBy` | ~30 (1 loop, 1 assignment, several invocations) | 1 | 1 |
| `validateDamages` | ~35 (1 loop, 1 conditional/if, 1 throw, 2 invocations of countBy) | 2 | 2 |
| `sumDamagePayouts` | ~12 | 1 | 1 |
| `processClaim` | ~28 (several invocations, 1 assignment via -=) | 1 | 1 |
| `processStep` | ~10 (1 conditional ternary) | 2 | 1 |
| `processScenario` | ~26 (1 loop via map, several invocations, assignments) | 1 | 1 |

**Worst-McCabe target candidates**: McCabe maxes at 2 across the whole file. **Worst APP mass** is `validateDamages` (~35) followed by `countBy` (~30) and `processClaim` (~28).

Let me look closer at `validateDamages` and `damagePayout` because:
- `damagePayout` uses `as Item` cast (a code smell hiding incomplete typing) and assumes `find` returns a value
- `processClaim` also uses `as Policy` cast
- `validateDamages` has a `throw` inside a loop and uses two passes of `countBy`

Let me consider naming evaluation first.

**Naming Evaluation:**
- `damagePayout`: Returns payout for a single damage. Name is fine.
- `validateDamages`: Validates that damage counts don't exceed item counts. Name is fine.
- `processClaim`: Processes a claim step. Fine.
- `countBy`: Returns counts grouped by a key. Fine.
- `policyBase`: Returns base premium. Fine.

Names look good. Now the highest-impact target.

Looking at the code carefully, I notice an opportunity around **`damagePayout` and `processClaim`** where a type cast `as Item` and `as Policy` indicates a smell. But these are working code currently — refactoring types is risky.

Let me look at the most complex function `validateDamages` — it has highest mass and uses two separate `countBy` invocations. The validation could be simplified.

Actually, looking again at `processClaim`:

```typescript
const processClaim = (step: ClaimStep, state: ScenarioState): ClaimResult => {
  const policy = state.policies.get(step.policy) as Policy;
  validateDamages(step.incident.damages, policy.items);
  const rawPayout = sumDamagePayouts(step.incident.damages, policy.items);
  const payout = Math.floor(Math.min(rawPayout, policy.remainingCap));
  policy.remainingCap -= payout;
  return { payout, remainingCap: policy.remainingCap };
};
```

There's repetition of `step.incident.damages` and `policy.items`. Plus `policy.remainingCap` appears 3 times. The `as Policy` cast suggests the implementation should be more defensive — but the test for unknown policy doesn't exist yet.

Let me look at one clear refactoring opportunity: the **`isCompleteComponentBlock`** function uses `BLOCK_SIZE` but `groupTotal` reads cleaner if we inline naming. Actually that's already clean.

A clearer angle: `validateDamages` mutates via `throw` inside a loop. Could we extract a `findExceedingDamage` query? Let's measure that change:

Current `validateDamages` (lines 219-229):
- Bindings: itemCounts, damageCounts, type, count = 4
- Invocations: countBy×2, get, throw `new Error` = 4 (template literal)
- Loops: 1 (for-of)
- Conditionals: 1 (if)
- Constants: 1 (string template)
- Mass: 4 + 8 + 5 + 4 + 1 = ~22

Hmm, my prior estimate was off. Let me look at the cleanest single refactoring target.

Looking again: there's a subtle duplication. `damagePayout` and `processClaim` both pass `policy.items`. Could we precompute or improve structure?

Actually, the most impactful refactoring would be **extracting the find-or-throw pattern** in `damagePayout`. Currently:

```typescript
const item = items.find((it) => it.type === damage.itemType) as Item;
```

The `as Item` cast is a smell — `find` returns `Item | undefined`. We've already validated via `validateDamages` that the type exists, so this is "safe", but the cast obscures this invariant.

However, refactoring this is risky if it changes behavior. Let me instead look at a **higher impact** opportunity: the `processClaim` function has multiple concerns. Let me extract a small helper.

Actually, looking at all the metrics, the codebase is already in excellent shape. The McCabe max is 2, cognitive max is 2, and ESLint is clean. Let me look for one thing I can genuinely improve.

**Opportunity identified**: In `validateDamages`, the validation builds a full `itemCounts` map upfront, which iterates over all items. But for a typical case where damages list is small, we only need counts for the types that appear in damages. More importantly, `damageCounts` is a separate construction.

But a simpler win: the **`countBy` helper** is only used twice, both in `validateDamages`, and both calls inline create a `key` function. We can inline `countBy` or simplify.

Actually wait — let me look for **conceptual duplication**. In `groupByType` (lines 98-106) and `countBy` (lines 210-217), there's a very similar pattern: iterate items, maintain a map keyed by some property. Could we unify?

```typescript
// groupByType: groups items by type into Map<string, Item[]>
// countBy: counts items by key into Map<string, number>
```

These have *structurally* similar shape but different value semantics (array vs count). Unifying might hurt clarity (Rule 2 trumps DRY).

Let me look for something more concrete. Let me re-examine to find one specific refactoring that has measurable impact.

**One concrete change**: In `processClaim`, the variable `rawPayout` is used once. We can inline it for fewer elements (Rule 4):

```typescript
// Before
const rawPayout = sumDamagePayouts(step.incident.damages, policy.items);
const payout = Math.floor(Math.min(rawPayout, policy.remainingCap));

// After
const payout = Math.floor(
  Math.min(sumDamagePayouts(step.incident.damages, policy.items), policy.remainingCap),
);
```

But this hurts clarity (Rule 2 > Rule 4). Skip.

**Better target**: `validateDamages` uses a `for...of` loop with side-effect throwing. Refactoring to a functional style using `Array.from(map)` doesn't improve much.

Looking once more at the code, I see that `damagePayout` has a hidden assumption: the item exists in `items`. This is guarded externally by `validateDamages`. Let me consider if we can make this assumption explicit.

Actually, I think the cleanest refactoring is to **consolidate item lookup** so both validation and payout use the same iteration pattern. But this is a structural change.

Let me try a smaller and very concrete refactoring: **rename `damagePayout` to `damageReimbursement`** — wait, current name is fine.

After this careful analysis, I think the implementation is genuinely close to its floor. Let me look for one more angle: the `groupTotal` short-circuit logic could be cleaner.

Actually, let me look at **`isCompleteComponentBlock`** and `groupTotal`:

```typescript
const isCompleteComponentBlock = (type: string, group: Item[]): boolean =>
  group.length === BLOCK_SIZE && COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type);

const groupTotal = (type: string, group: Item[]): number =>
  isCompleteComponentBlock(type, group)
    ? COMPONENT_BLOCK_PRICE
    : sumBasePremiums(group);
```

`isCompleteComponentBlock` is only called from `groupTotal`. Could merge — but two well-named functions is clearer than one. Rule 2 stays.

Let me try a different angle. Looking at `processClaim`:

```typescript
const processClaim = (step: ClaimStep, state: ScenarioState): ClaimResult => {
  const policy = state.policies.get(step.policy) as Policy;
  validateDamages(step.incident.damages, policy.items);
  const rawPayout = sumDamagePayouts(step.incident.damages, policy.items);
  const payout = Math.floor(Math.min(rawPayout, policy.remainingCap));
  policy.remainingCap -= payout;
  return { payout, remainingCap: policy.remainingCap };
};
```

The pair `(step.incident.damages, policy.items)` appears twice. This is duplication (Rule 3). Let me destructure:
